does: This paper presents HAMSA, an automated red-teaming framework for generating jailbreak prompts against aligned compact language models. The method uses multi-stage evolutionary search with population-based optimization and temperature-controlled variability to iteratively refine candidate prompts. The approach produces semantically meaningful and fluent jailbreak prompts that maintain natural language quality while bypassing alignment safeguards, avoiding the incoherent obfuscated text that triggers perplexity-based filters. The framework is evaluated on English benchmarks (In-The-Wild Jailbreak Prompts) and on a newly created Arabic benchmark derived from similar sources, demonstrating systematic discovery of prompts that elicit harmful outputs from defended models.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on adversarial attacks against LLMs, building on prior manual jailbreak engineering efforts and rudimentary prompt obfuscation techniques that produced low-quality results. It responds to limitations in existing automated adversarial prompt generation methods by addressing the challenge of maintaining semantic coherence while evading perplexity filters—a key weakness of earlier approaches. The evolutionary search methodology draws on population-based optimization strategies from adversarial machine learning, adapting these techniques specifically for the challenge of jailbreaking compact, efficiency-oriented model variants that represent an increasingly deployed class of LLMs.
enables: This work enables more rigorous evaluation of alignment techniques in compact LLMs by providing sophisticated automated adversarial examples that researchers can use for red-teaming. The method points toward future research on developing stronger defenses against stealthy jailbreaks that maintain fluency, including improvements to safety training and detection mechanisms that look beyond simple perplexity metrics. By demonstrating vulnerabilities across multiple languages including Arabic, it highlights the need for multilingual alignment research and culturally-aware safety measures. The evolutionary framework could also inform broader automated adversarial testing pipelines and inspire investigation into why compact models remain particularly susceptible despite alignment efforts.
